data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider OGE Energy (OGE) After Its Strong Multi‑Year Share Price Run
data.description:
• If you have been wondering whether OGE Energy at around US$48.76 is still offering value, this article walks through the key numbers that matter before you make up your mind.
  • OGE Energy's share price recently closed at US$48.76, with returns of 2.6% over the last 7 days, 1.0% over the past month, 13.7% year to date and 16.1% over the last year, plus 42.7% over 3 years and 86.5% over 5 years.
  • These return figures have put the stock on the radar for investors weighing potential upside against the risk of buying after a strong run. Recent coverage has focused on how the company fits into the regulated utilities space and what that could mean for future capital allocation and dividend policy.
  • Simply Wall St currently assigns OGE Energy a valuation score of 2 out of 6. The next sections will compare what traditional metrics like P/E multiples and cash flow based models say about the current price, before circling back to a more complete way of thinking about valuation at the end of the article.

OGE Energy scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: OGE Energy Dividend Discount Model (DDM) Analysis

The Dividend Discount Model estimates what a share might be worth by projecting future dividends and the rate at which those dividends could grow, then converting those future payments into a single value today.

For OGE Energy, the model uses an annual dividend per share of US$1.76 and a payout ratio of 76.67%. With a return on equity of 9.79%, this implies an estimated dividend growth rate of about 2.28%. In this framework, the company is assumed to reinvest the portion of earnings not paid out and earn 9.79% on that amount, which drives the growth input in the model.

Based on these assumptions, the DDM output suggests an intrinsic value of about US$37.57 per share, compared with the recent share price of US$48.76. That difference suggests the stock could be around 29.8% overvalued when viewed strictly through the lens of the dividend and growth inputs used here.

This model is useful if the primary focus is dividend income, but it does not capture every aspect of the business or potential risks.

Result: OVERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for OGE Energy.

Approach 2: OGE Energy Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like OGE Energy, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand because it ties the share price directly to the earnings that support it. Investors typically accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger earnings growth or see the business as lower risk, and a lower P/E when growth is modest or risks are higher.

OGE Energy is trading on a P/E of 21.37x. That sits close to the broader Electric Utilities industry average of 21.87x and below the peer average of 27.55x. Simply Wall St also calculates a Fair Ratio of 19.76x, which reflects what the P/E might be based on factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio goes further than a simple comparison with peers or the industry because it adjusts for the company’s own profile rather than assuming all utilities deserve the same multiple. Comparing the current 21.37x P/E with the Fair Ratio of 19.76x suggests OGE Energy is trading at a premium to what the model implies.

Result: OVERVALUED

What Is Mattel's Investment Narrative?

For Mattel, the investment case really comes down to whether you believe the company can turn a broad portfolio of brands and licenses into consistent, profitable cash generation, despite modest revenue growth and shrinking margins in 2025. The new Masters of the Universe film tie-in fits directly into that thesis: it extends Mattel further into entertainment-driven franchises and high-margin licensing, and could support nearer-term sentiment if early sell-through and audience engagement land well. That said, the share price has slid sharply year to date even as buybacks continue, reflecting investor focus on slowing earnings, high debt and questions around capital allocation, including CEO pay that rose while profits fell. In this context, Masters of the Universe is more of a potential catalyst than a fix for the underlying risks.
